name: pharmacy_robot_dose_substitution
premise: Sofia Marin, director of pharmacy at St. Agnes Medical Center, must decide whether to shut down a robotic medication dispensing line after a rare substitution pattern is detected. The robot has reduced medication turnaround time by 46% during a nurse shortage and is especially important for chemotherapy and neonatal dosing. A pharmacist discovers that when two look-alike vials have partially damaged barcodes, the robot sometimes uses shelf-position confidence to complete verification. The vendor estimates the risk at fewer than one in 20,000 dispenses, but internal logs show barcode damage is more common in the oncology refrigerator because condensation blurs labels. Shutting down the robot will delay time-sensitive chemotherapy and force exhausted pharmacists into manual fills. Keeping it active preserves access but risks a wrong-dose event in precisely the departments least able to tolerate error.

agents.name: Sofia Marin
agents.identity: I am the hospital pharmacy director responsible for medication safety and continuity of critical drug preparation.
agents.goal: I want to protect patients from dispensing errors while avoiding treatment delays for fragile patients.

agents.name: Dr. Owen Kline
agents.identity: I am the oncology service chief whose patients depend on timely chemotherapy prepared by the robotic line.
agents.goal: I want my patients to receive scheduled treatment without avoidable delays, while still respecting medication safety limits.

choices: I will shut down the robotic dispensing line for oncology and neonatal medications until barcode verification is repaired, accepting treatment delays and manual workload risks.
I will keep the robot active with pharmacist double-checks for damaged barcodes, accepting that condensation-related label errors may still evade detection.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Sofia Marin must decide whether to suspend the robotic line or continue using it with added human checks. What does Sofia Marin do?
